Outcomes.Text: Explain the structure and advantages of NumPy arrays over standard Python lists for engineering computation.
Create NumPy arrays using a variety of construction methods and initialization functions.
Apply element-wise operations and broadcasting rules to perform efficient array arithmetic.
Demonstrate indexing, slicing, reshaping, and transposing techniques to access and manipulate array data.
Analyze engineering problems using NumPy linear algebra routines and built-in mathematical functions.
Evaluate datasets by applying NumPy statistical functions to summarize and interpret numerical results.

Topics.Title: Introduction to NumPy and the ndarray
Topics.Summary: Covers what NumPy is, why it is the cornerstone of numerical engineering computation in Python, and how the ndarray differs fundamentally from a Python list.

Elements.Title: What Is NumPy?
Elements.BodyText: NumPy is an open-source Python library that provides a high-performance multidimensional array object and a collection of routines for fast mathematical operations on those arrays.
Elements.Notes: NumPy stands for Numerical Python. It was originally developed as Numeric and later merged with Numarray to become NumPy. It underpins virtually every scientific Python library, including SciPy, pandas, and TensorFlow.

Items.Text: NumPy is the foundational numerical computing library for the Python scientific ecosystem.
It is implemented primarily in C, making array operations significantly faster than pure Python loops.
Most major engineering and data-science libraries depend on NumPy as a core dependency.

Elements.Title: The ndarray Object
Elements.BodyText: The ndarray (n-dimensional array) is NumPy's primary data structure, storing elements of a single data type in a contiguous block of memory.
Elements.Notes: Unlike Python lists, all elements in an ndarray share the same dtype. This homogeneity enables vectorized computation and avoids the overhead of type-checking at runtime.

Items.Text: An ndarray has a fixed size at creation; changing its size creates a new array.
Every ndarray carries attributes: shape, dtype, ndim, and size.
Homogeneous data types allow SIMD and vectorized CPU instructions to accelerate computation.

Elements.Title: NumPy Arrays vs. Python Lists
Elements.BodyText: While Python lists are flexible containers that can hold mixed types, NumPy arrays are typed, contiguous, and designed for numerical batch operations.
Elements.Notes: A benchmark comparison is instructive: summing one million numbers with a Python loop typically takes tens of milliseconds, while the NumPy equivalent takes microseconds.

Items.Text: Python lists support mixed types; NumPy arrays enforce a single dtype for all elements.
Arithmetic on NumPy arrays is vectorized and requires no explicit loops.
NumPy arrays consume less memory than equivalent Python lists due to fixed-size typed storage.
Broadcasting and ufuncs are unavailable on plain Python lists.

Elements.Title: Key Array Attributes
Elements.BodyText: Understanding an array's metadata attributes is essential before performing any computation or manipulation.
Elements.Notes: Use arr.shape to confirm dimensions before matrix operations, arr.dtype to avoid precision errors, and arr.ndim to distinguish vectors from matrices from higher-rank tensors.

Items.Text: shape returns a tuple indicating the size along each dimension.
dtype describes the data type of stored elements, such as float64 or int32.
ndim gives the number of dimensions (axes) in the array.
size returns the total number of elements across all dimensions.

Topics.Title: Creating NumPy Arrays
Topics.Summary: Explores the full range of array construction methods, from converting Python sequences to using specialized initialization functions that generate arrays of specific values or ranges.

Elements.Title: Creating Arrays from Python Sequences
Elements.BodyText: The most direct way to create a NumPy array is by passing a Python list or tuple to np.array(), which infers the shape and dtype automatically.
Elements.Notes: Nested lists produce multidimensional arrays. For example, np.array([[1,2],[3,4]]) produces a 2×2 integer array. Explicitly specifying dtype=float64 avoids unintended integer truncation.

Items.Text: np.array() accepts any sequence-like object, including lists, tuples, and nested combinations.
The dtype argument can be specified explicitly to control precision.
Nested lists with uniform inner lengths automatically produce 2-D or higher arrays.

Elements.Title: Range and Linspace Functions
Elements.BodyText: np.arange() and np.linspace() generate arrays of evenly spaced values and are essential for creating domain variables in engineering simulations.
Elements.Notes: np.arange(start, stop, step) mirrors Python's range but returns an ndarray. np.linspace(start, stop, num) guarantees exactly num points and is preferred when a specific count is needed rather than a specific step size.

Items.Text: np.arange(start, stop, step) generates values at fixed step intervals.
np.linspace(start, stop, num) generates a fixed number of evenly spaced points including both endpoints by default.
For floating-point steps, linspace is safer than arange to avoid accumulation errors.
Both functions return 1-D arrays that can be reshaped afterward.

Elements.Title: Constant-Value Initialization Functions
Elements.BodyText: NumPy provides convenience functions to create arrays pre-filled with zeros, ones, or arbitrary constants, which are commonly needed to initialize result buffers in iterative engineering algorithms.
Elements.Notes: np.zeros_like() and np.ones_like() create arrays matching the shape and dtype of an existing array, which is handy when building output arrays inside functions.

Items.Text: np.zeros(shape) creates an array filled with 0.0 of the specified shape.
np.ones(shape) creates an array filled with 1.0.
np.full(shape, fill_value) fills the array with any scalar constant.
np.empty(shape) allocates memory without initialization, offering a small speed advantage when values will be overwritten immediately.

Elements.Title: Identity and Diagonal Matrices
Elements.BodyText: For linear algebra workflows, NumPy provides direct constructors for identity and diagonal matrices that appear frequently in engineering mathematics.
Elements.Notes: np.eye(n) is more general than np.identity(n), allowing rectangular matrices via the k offset parameter for the diagonal. np.diag() is bidirectional: it extracts a diagonal from a matrix or constructs a matrix from a 1-D array.

Items.Text: np.eye(n) creates an n×n identity matrix.
np.diag(v) constructs a square diagonal matrix from a 1-D array v.
When passed a 2-D array, np.diag() extracts the diagonal as a 1-D array.
The k parameter in np.eye() shifts the diagonal above or below the main diagonal.

Elements.Title: Random Array Generation
Elements.BodyText: NumPy's random module enables generation of arrays populated with random values drawn from various statistical distributions, critical for Monte Carlo simulations and testing.
Elements.Notes: Use np.random.seed() or a Generator object (np.random.default_rng()) for reproducible results, which is essential in engineering validation workflows.

Items.Text: np.random.rand(shape) draws samples from a uniform distribution over [0, 1).
np.random.randn(shape) draws from the standard normal distribution.
np.random.randint(low, high, size) generates random integers.
Setting a random seed ensures reproducible results across runs.

Topics.Title: Element-Wise Operations and Universal Functions
Topics.Summary: Describes how NumPy performs arithmetic and mathematical operations element-by-element using ufuncs, enabling vectorized computation without explicit Python loops.

Elements.Title: Vectorized Arithmetic
Elements.BodyText: Arithmetic operators applied to NumPy arrays act element-wise, computing operations across all elements simultaneously without any Python-level loop.
Elements.Notes: This is sometimes called vectorization. It delegates the looping to optimized C code, typically achieving speedups of 10–100× over equivalent Python loops for large arrays.

Items.Text: Operators +, -, *, /, **, and // are applied element-wise between same-shaped arrays.
Operations between an array and a scalar apply the scalar to every element.
Element-wise operations never modify the original array unless an in-place operator (+=, *=) is used.
Results have the same shape as the input arrays.

Elements.Title: Universal Functions (ufuncs)
Elements.BodyText: Ufuncs are NumPy functions that operate element-wise on arrays and support additional features such as output array specification and reduction methods.
Elements.Notes: Examples of ufuncs include np.sqrt(), np.exp(), np.log(), np.sin(), and np.abs(). All accept the optional out= parameter to write results into a pre-allocated array, avoiding extra memory allocation.

Items.Text: Ufuncs apply a mathematical operation element-wise across an entire array in a single call.
They are implemented in compiled C and are significantly faster than equivalent Python math function calls in a loop.
Common ufuncs include np.sqrt, np.exp, np.log, np.sin, np.cos, and np.abs.
The out= keyword argument directs output to an existing array, reducing memory overhead.

Elements.Title: Comparison and Boolean Operations
Elements.BodyText: Comparison operators applied to arrays return boolean arrays of the same shape, which can be used directly for filtering and conditional logic.
Elements.Notes: Use np.any() and np.all() to reduce a boolean array to a scalar. Avoid using Python's built-in any() and all() on arrays, as they do not handle multidimensional arrays correctly.

Items.Text: Operators <, >, ==, !=, <=, >= return boolean ndarrays element-wise.
Boolean arrays can be used as masks to select elements satisfying a condition.
np.any() returns True if at least one element is True; np.all() requires all elements to be True.
Logical operators np.logical_and(), np.logical_or(), and np.logical_not() combine boolean arrays.

Elements.Title: Aggregate Reduction Functions
Elements.BodyText: NumPy provides functions that reduce an array along one or more axes, computing values such as sum, product, minimum, and maximum.
Elements.Notes: The axis parameter is critical: axis=0 reduces along rows (collapsing to one row), axis=1 reduces along columns (collapsing to one column), and omitting axis reduces the entire array to a scalar.

Items.Text: np.sum(), np.prod(), np.min(), and np.max() reduce an array to a scalar or lower-rank array.
The axis parameter controls which dimension is collapsed.
np.cumsum() and np.cumprod() return arrays of running totals rather than a single aggregate.
keepdims=True preserves the collapsed dimension as size-1, which aids broadcasting in subsequent operations.

Topics.Title: Broadcasting
Topics.Summary: Explains NumPy's broadcasting mechanism that allows arithmetic operations between arrays of different but compatible shapes without copying data.

Elements.Title: What Is Broadcasting?
Elements.BodyText: Broadcasting is NumPy's set of rules for stretching arrays with smaller shapes to match larger arrays during arithmetic, enabling operations between arrays of different dimensions.
Elements.Notes: Broadcasting does not physically copy data; it uses strides to simulate the expanded array. This makes it both memory-efficient and fast. The concept originates in APL and has been adopted by most modern numerical computing frameworks.

Items.Text: Broadcasting allows operations between arrays with different but compatible shapes.
No data is copied; NumPy uses internal stride tricks to simulate shape expansion.
Broadcasting is applied automatically when standard arithmetic operators are used.

Elements.Title: Broadcasting Rules
Elements.BodyText: NumPy aligns shapes from the trailing (rightmost) dimension and applies three rules to determine whether two arrays can be broadcast together.
Elements.Notes: Rule 1: Prepend 1s to the shorter shape. Rule 2: Dimensions of size 1 are stretched to match the other array. Rule 3: If sizes differ and neither is 1, a ValueError is raised. Example: (3,1) and (1,4) broadcast to (3,4).

Items.Text: Shapes are compared element-wise starting from the trailing dimension.
A dimension of size 1 in either array is stretched to match the other array's size in that dimension.
If two dimensions are neither equal nor one of them is 1, the arrays are incompatible and a ValueError is raised.
Arrays with fewer dimensions are treated as if padded with leading 1s.

Elements.Title: Common Broadcasting Patterns in Engineering
Elements.BodyText: Broadcasting naturally handles many recurring engineering patterns, such as subtracting a mean vector from every row of a matrix or scaling columns by different factors.
Elements.Notes: A typical example: subtracting the column-wise mean from a data matrix of shape (N, M) using a mean array of shape (1, M). Broadcasting stretches the mean across all N rows without a loop.

Items.Text: Adding a 1-D array of length M to a 2-D array of shape (N, M) subtracts or adds values column-wise.
Scaling each column of a matrix by a different factor requires a shape-(M,) array.
Outer products can be computed by reshaping two 1-D arrays to (N,1) and (1,M) and multiplying.
Normalizing each row of a matrix uses a column vector of shape (N,1).

Elements.Title: Avoiding Broadcasting Errors
Elements.BodyText: Mismatched shapes are a frequent source of bugs; understanding shape mismatches and how to inspect and fix them prevents runtime errors.
Elements.Notes: Always print arr.shape before broadcasting in debugging sessions. np.newaxis (equivalent to None) is the standard way to insert a new size-1 dimension to make shapes compatible.

Items.Text: Inspect arr.shape before combining arrays to anticipate broadcasting behavior.
Use np.newaxis or reshape to insert size-1 dimensions and align shapes.
A ValueError with 'could not broadcast' indicates incompatible non-unity dimensions.
Use np.broadcast_shapes() to check compatibility without executing the operation.

Topics.Title: Indexing, Slicing, and Fancy Indexing
Topics.Summary: Covers all methods for accessing and selecting subsets of array elements, from basic integer indexing and slices to advanced Boolean and integer-array indexing.

Elements.Title: Basic Integer Indexing
Elements.BodyText: Individual array elements are accessed with integer indices, using zero-based indexing and supporting negative indices to count from the end.
Elements.Notes: For a 2-D array arr, arr[i, j] is equivalent to arr[i][j] but is preferred because it avoids creating an intermediate sub-array. Negative indices wrap around: arr[-1] is the last element.

Items.Text: NumPy uses zero-based indexing; the first element is at index 0.
Negative indices count from the end: -1 is the last element, -2 is second-to-last.
Multi-dimensional arrays use comma-separated indices: arr[row, col].
Accessing a single element returns a scalar, not a 0-D array.

Elements.Title: Slicing Arrays
Elements.BodyText: Slices extract contiguous subarrays using the start:stop:step syntax and return a view into the original array, not a copy.
Elements.Notes: Because slices are views, modifying a slice modifies the original array. Use arr[start:stop].copy() when an independent copy is required. This view behavior is intentional for memory efficiency but can cause subtle bugs.

Items.Text: Slicing syntax is arr[start:stop:step], where any component can be omitted.
Slices return a view; modifications to the slice affect the original array.
Each dimension of a multi-dimensional array can be sliced independently with commas.
Use .copy() to obtain an independent copy when the original must not be modified.

Elements.Title: Boolean (Mask) Indexing
Elements.BodyText: A boolean array of the same shape as the target can be used as an index to select elements where the mask is True, returning a flattened 1-D array of matching elements.
Elements.Notes: Boolean indexing always returns a copy, not a view. This is useful for filtering sensor readings above a threshold or replacing NaN values, for example: arr[arr < 0] = 0 to clip negatives.

Items.Text: A boolean mask of the same shape selects only elements where the mask is True.
Boolean indexing returns a copy, not a view of the original array.
Conditions can be combined with & (and), | (or), and ~ (not) for multi-criteria filters.
Assignment through a boolean mask modifies elements in-place: arr[mask] = value.

Elements.Title: Fancy (Integer Array) Indexing
Elements.BodyText: Fancy indexing uses an array of integer indices to select arbitrary elements or rows in any order, enabling non-contiguous and reordered selections.
Elements.Notes: Unlike slices, fancy indexing always returns a copy. It is commonly used to reorder rows of a matrix or sample random rows from a dataset.

Items.Text: Passing an integer array as an index selects elements at those positions in any order.
Fancy indexing on a 2-D array with row and column index arrays selects element pairs.
Results are always copies, never views.
np.ix_() constructs open meshes from multiple 1-D index arrays for submatrix selection.

Elements.Title: np.where and Conditional Selection
Elements.BodyText: np.where() provides a vectorized conditional expression that selects elements from two arrays based on a boolean condition, similar to a ternary operator applied element-wise.
Elements.Notes: np.where(condition, x, y) returns elements from x where condition is True and from y elsewhere. When called with only a condition, it returns the indices where the condition is True, equivalent to np.nonzero().

Items.Text: np.where(condition, x, y) returns element-wise selection between x and y based on condition.
Both x and y can be arrays or scalars and are subject to broadcasting.
np.where(condition) returns a tuple of index arrays where condition is True.
It is often faster and more readable than equivalent Python conditional list comprehensions.

Topics.Title: Reshaping, Transposing, and Array Manipulation
Topics.Summary: Explains how to change the shape and layout of arrays without altering their data, including reshape, transpose, flatten, and stacking operations fundamental to preparing data for engineering algorithms.

Elements.Title: Reshaping Arrays
Elements.BodyText: The reshape() method returns a new view of the array with a different shape, provided the total number of elements remains the same.
Elements.Notes: Using -1 as one dimension argument lets NumPy infer that dimension automatically. For example, arr.reshape(-1, 3) on an array of 12 elements produces a (4,3) array. reshape() returns a view when possible; use np.reshape() or .copy() if ownership matters.

Items.Text: reshape(new_shape) returns a view with the specified shape if total element count is preserved.
Using -1 for one dimension allows NumPy to compute that dimension automatically.
Reshaping does not copy data when the array is contiguous in memory.
Chaining reshape calls enables flexible data preparation pipelines.

Elements.Title: Transposing Arrays
Elements.BodyText: Transposition permutes the axes of an array; for 2-D arrays this swaps rows and columns, and is accessed via the .T attribute or np.transpose().
Elements.Notes: For arrays with more than two dimensions, np.transpose(arr, axes) specifies a custom axis permutation. Note that .T on a 1-D array has no effect because there is only one axis.

Items.Text: arr.T returns the transpose, swapping all pairs of axes for a 2-D array.
np.transpose(arr, axes) specifies a custom permutation of axes for N-D arrays.
Transpose returns a view, so no data is copied.
Transposing is essential before matrix multiplication when dimension alignment is needed.

Elements.Title: Flattening and Raveling
Elements.BodyText: flatten() and ravel() both return 1-D versions of a multidimensional array, but differ in whether they return a copy or a view.
Elements.Notes: ravel() returns a view whenever possible (when the array is C-contiguous), making it faster. flatten() always returns a copy. In practice, ravel() is preferred in performance-critical code.

Items.Text: flatten() always returns a 1-D copy of the array.
ravel() returns a 1-D view when possible, otherwise a copy.
Both traverse elements in row-major (C) order by default.
The order parameter ('C', 'F', 'A') controls traversal order for column-major (Fortran) layouts.

Elements.Title: Stacking and Splitting Arrays
Elements.BodyText: NumPy provides functions to combine multiple arrays along existing or new axes, and to split a single array into multiple sub-arrays.
Elements.Notes: np.vstack() is equivalent to np.concatenate(..., axis=0) for 2-D arrays. np.hstack() concatenates along axis=1. np.stack() introduces a new axis. np.split() divides along an axis at specified indices.

Items.Text: np.concatenate([a, b], axis) joins arrays along an existing axis.
np.vstack() stacks arrays vertically (row-wise); np.hstack() stacks horizontally (column-wise).
np.stack() joins arrays along a new axis, increasing ndim by one.
np.split(), np.vsplit(), and np.hsplit() divide arrays into sub-arrays.

Elements.Title: Adding and Removing Dimensions
Elements.BodyText: np.newaxis and np.expand_dims() insert size-1 dimensions to adjust array rank for broadcasting or function compatibility.
Elements.Notes: arr[:, np.newaxis] converts a shape-(N,) array to shape-(N,1), making it a column vector. np.squeeze() removes all size-1 dimensions, which is the inverse operation.

Items.Text: np.newaxis inserts a new size-1 axis at the specified position.
np.expand_dims(arr, axis) is a named-argument alternative to np.newaxis.
np.squeeze() removes all dimensions of size 1 from an array.
These operations are views and do not copy data.

Topics.Title: Linear Algebra with NumPy
Topics.Summary: Covers NumPy's linear algebra capabilities, including matrix multiplication, decompositions, solving linear systems, and computing norms and determinants as needed for engineering analysis.

Elements.Title: Matrix Multiplication
Elements.BodyText: NumPy supports matrix multiplication via the @ operator and np.matmul(), which perform the standard dot product across rows and columns, distinct from element-wise multiplication.
Elements.Notes: The * operator always performs element-wise multiplication. The @ operator was introduced in Python 3.5 (PEP 465) and is preferred over np.dot() for clarity in matrix expressions. np.dot() has subtly different behavior for arrays with more than two dimensions.

Items.Text: The @ operator performs standard matrix multiplication between 2-D arrays.
np.matmul() is equivalent to @ and also handles batched matrix multiplication for N-D arrays.
For 1-D arrays, @ computes the inner (dot) product and returns a scalar.
Element-wise multiplication uses * and is distinct from matrix multiplication.

Elements.Title: Solving Linear Systems
Elements.BodyText: np.linalg.solve(A, b) efficiently solves the linear system Ax = b for x using LU factorization, which is the standard approach for engineering systems of equations.
Elements.Notes: np.linalg.solve() is preferred over computing the matrix inverse (np.linalg.inv()) because it is more numerically stable and computationally efficient. Use np.linalg.lstsq() for overdetermined systems.

Items.Text: np.linalg.solve(A, b) returns x such that A @ x == b.
A must be a square non-singular matrix; otherwise a LinAlgError is raised.
This is numerically preferable to computing A_inv @ b via np.linalg.inv().
np.linalg.lstsq() handles overdetermined systems using least squares minimization.

Elements.Title: Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
Elements.BodyText: np.linalg.eig() and np.linalg.eigh() compute eigenvalues and eigenvectors of square matrices, used extensively in structural analysis, vibration problems, and principal component analysis.
Elements.Notes: Use np.linalg.eigh() (Hermitian/symmetric) rather than np.linalg.eig() for symmetric matrices, as eigh guarantees real-valued eigenvalues and is faster and more numerically stable.

Items.Text: np.linalg.eig(A) returns eigenvalues and right eigenvectors of a general square matrix.
np.linalg.eigh(A) is optimized for symmetric or Hermitian matrices and returns real eigenvalues.
Eigenvalues are returned as a 1-D array; eigenvectors are columns of the returned 2-D array.
Eigenvalue decompositions appear in vibration analysis, stability studies, and dimensionality reduction.

Elements.Title: Matrix Decompositions
Elements.BodyText: NumPy provides LU (via solve), QR, SVD, and Cholesky decompositions through the np.linalg submodule, enabling factorization-based approaches to engineering computations.
Elements.Notes: SVD (np.linalg.svd) is the most general decomposition and works for any matrix shape. Cholesky (np.linalg.cholesky) is the most efficient for positive-definite systems such as covariance matrices.

Items.Text: np.linalg.qr(A) returns Q (orthogonal) and R (upper triangular) factors.
np.linalg.svd(A) returns U, singular values s, and Vh for any matrix shape.
np.linalg.cholesky(A) computes the Cholesky factor for symmetric positive-definite matrices.
Decompositions are numerically more stable than directly computing inverses or determinants.

Elements.Title: Norms and Determinants
Elements.BodyText: np.linalg.norm() computes vector and matrix norms used for convergence checks and condition assessment, while np.linalg.det() computes the scalar determinant.
Elements.Notes: The condition number (np.linalg.cond()) measures how sensitive a linear system is to numerical errors. A large condition number warns of near-singularity and potential numerical instability.

Items.Text: np.linalg.norm(v) computes the Euclidean (L2) norm of a vector by default.
The ord parameter selects different norms: 1-norm, infinity-norm, Frobenius norm for matrices.
np.linalg.det(A) returns the determinant of a square matrix.
np.linalg.cond(A) returns the condition number, indicating numerical stability of the system.

Topics.Title: Statistical and Mathematical Functions
Topics.Summary: Introduces NumPy's built-in statistical descriptors and mathematical functions that enable rapid characterization of engineering data and evaluation of mathematical expressions over arrays.

Elements.Title: Descriptive Statistics
Elements.BodyText: NumPy provides array-level functions for computing mean, median, variance, and standard deviation along specified axes, forming the basis for engineering data characterization.
Elements.Notes: np.mean() and np.std() use N in the denominator by default (population statistics). Set ddof=1 for sample statistics (N−1 denominator), which is standard in experimental data analysis.

Items.Text: np.mean(arr, axis) computes the arithmetic mean along the specified axis.
np.median(arr) finds the middle value and is more robust to outliers than the mean.
np.std(arr, ddof=1) computes the sample standard deviation with Bessel's correction.
np.var(arr) computes variance; set ddof=1 for sample variance.

Elements.Title: Min, Max, and Sorting
Elements.BodyText: Functions for finding extrema and sorting arrays are fundamental to data analysis, threshold detection, and ranked ordering of engineering results.
Elements.Notes: np.argmin() and np.argmax() return indices rather than values, which is useful for locating the time step or position of a peak in a signal. np.argsort() returns an index array that would sort the input.

Items.Text: np.min() and np.max() find the minimum and maximum values along an axis.
np.argmin() and np.argmax() return the indices of the minimum and maximum values.
np.sort() returns a sorted copy; np.argsort() returns the sorting indices.
np.percentile() and np.quantile() compute percentile-based thresholds from a dataset.

Elements.Title: Trigonometric and Exponential Functions
Elements.BodyText: NumPy includes a comprehensive set of element-wise mathematical functions for trigonometry, exponentials, and logarithms, essential for evaluating engineering formulas over arrays.
Elements.Notes: All NumPy trigonometric functions operate in radians. Use np.deg2rad() and np.rad2deg() to convert between degrees and radians. np.log() is natural log; np.log10() and np.log2() provide base-10 and base-2 logarithms.

Items.Text: np.sin(), np.cos(), and np.tan() operate in radians on element arrays.
np.exp() computes e raised to the power of each element.
np.log() computes the natural logarithm; np.log10() and np.log2() provide alternative bases.
np.deg2rad() and np.rad2deg() convert between degree and radian representations.

Elements.Title: Correlation and Covariance
Elements.BodyText: np.corrcoef() and np.cov() compute correlation and covariance matrices between variables, enabling assessment of relationships in multi-channel engineering data.
Elements.Notes: np.cov() expects each variable as a row and each observation as a column (shape M×N for M variables and N observations). Set rowvar=False to pass data in the transposed orientation.

Items.Text: np.corrcoef(X) returns the Pearson correlation coefficient matrix for rows of X.
np.cov(X) returns the covariance matrix; diagonal entries are variances.
A correlation value near ±1 indicates strong linear dependence between two variables.
These functions support ddof to switch between population and sample estimators.

Elements.Title: Polynomial Evaluation and Fitting
Elements.BodyText: np.polyval() evaluates a polynomial with given coefficients over an array of values, and np.polyfit() fits a least-squares polynomial to data, supporting common engineering curve-fitting tasks.
Elements.Notes: Coefficients returned by np.polyfit() are ordered from highest to lowest degree. For more robust polynomial fitting, the numpy.polynomial submodule (e.g., Polynomial.fit) is recommended for high-degree polynomials due to better numerical conditioning.

Items.Text: np.polyfit(x, y, deg) returns polynomial coefficients that minimize squared residuals.
np.polyval(coefficients, x) evaluates the polynomial at each point in x.
Coefficients are ordered from the highest degree term to the constant term.
For high-degree polynomials, np.polynomial.polynomial.Polynomial offers better numerical stability.

Elements.Title: Mathematical and Arithmetic Functions
Elements.BodyText: NumPy provides a rich set of universal functions (ufuncs) for performing element-wise mathematical operations on arrays. These include basic arithmetic as well as advanced mathematical computations commonly used in engineering analysis.
Elements.Notes: Examples include np.sqrt(), np.exp(), np.log(), np.sin(), np.cos(), and np.tan(). These functions operate efficiently on entire arrays without the need for explicit loops.

Items.Text: Universal functions (ufuncs) apply operations element-wise across entire arrays, making them highly efficient for large datasets.
Trigonometric functions such as np.sin(), np.cos(), and np.tan() accept angles in radians by default.
Exponential and logarithmic functions like np.exp() and np.log() are essential for signal processing and thermodynamic calculations.
Aggregate functions such as np.sum(), np.prod(), np.min(), and np.max() reduce arrays along specified axes.
Rounding functions including np.floor(), np.ceil(), and np.round() help control numerical precision in engineering outputs.

Elements.Title: Statistical Functions
Elements.BodyText: NumPy includes a comprehensive suite of statistical functions that allow engineers to analyze datasets, compute descriptive statistics, and understand data distributions. These functions operate directly on arrays and support axis-based computations.
Elements.Notes: For example, np.mean(data, axis=0) computes the column-wise mean of a 2D dataset, which is useful in sensor data analysis or material property averaging.

Items.Text: np.mean() and np.median() compute the average and middle value of an array, respectively, providing measures of central tendency.
np.std() and np.var() calculate the standard deviation and variance, quantifying the spread of engineering measurement data.
np.percentile() and np.quantile() identify threshold values within a distribution, useful for quality control and tolerance analysis.
np.corrcoef() computes the Pearson correlation matrix between variables, supporting multivariate engineering analysis.
All statistical functions accept an axis parameter, enabling row-wise or column-wise computation on multi-dimensional arrays.

Elements.Title: Linear Algebra Functions
Elements.BodyText: NumPy's numpy.linalg module provides essential linear algebra operations used extensively in structural analysis, control systems, and signal processing. These functions handle matrix operations that are fundamental to solving engineering problems.
Elements.Notes: For instance, np.linalg.solve(A, b) efficiently solves the system of linear equations Ax = b, which appears in finite element analysis and circuit network problems.

Items.Text: np.dot() and np.matmul() perform dot products and matrix multiplication, forming the basis of many engineering computations.
np.linalg.inv() computes the inverse of a square matrix, though np.linalg.solve() is preferred for solving linear systems due to better numerical stability.
np.linalg.eig() and np.linalg.eigh() compute eigenvalues and eigenvectors, critical for modal analysis and stability assessments.
np.linalg.svd() performs Singular Value Decomposition, used in data compression, signal processing, and least-squares problems.
np.linalg.det() calculates the determinant of a matrix, which indicates whether a system of equations has a unique solution.
np.linalg.norm() computes vector and matrix norms, providing measures of magnitude used in convergence checks and error analysis.

Elements.Title: Array Manipulation Functions
Elements.BodyText: NumPy offers a wide range of functions for reshaping, joining, splitting, and transforming arrays, enabling engineers to prepare and restructure data for computational workflows. These functions are essential for adapting data to the required format of algorithms and simulations.
Elements.Notes: A common engineering use case is reshaping a 1D array of sensor readings into a 2D matrix with np.reshape() so that rows represent time steps and columns represent sensor channels.

Items.Text: np.reshape() changes the shape of an array without altering its data, provided the total number of elements remains constant.
np.concatenate(), np.hstack(), and np.vstack() join multiple arrays along specified axes to combine datasets.
np.split(), np.hsplit(), and np.vsplit() divide arrays into sub-arrays, useful for partitioning datasets into training and testing sets.
np.transpose() and np.swapaxes() reorder array dimensions, often required when interfacing with different libraries or hardware data formats.
np.flatten() and np.ravel() convert multi-dimensional arrays into 1D arrays, with ravel returning a view when possible for memory efficiency.

Elements.Title: Sorting and Searching Functions
Elements.BodyText: NumPy provides functions to sort array elements and search for values meeting specific criteria, which are frequently needed in data preprocessing and engineering signal analysis. These functions support multi-dimensional arrays and offer control over sorting algorithms.
Elements.Notes: For example, np.argsort() returns the indices that would sort an array, allowing engineers to rank experimental measurements or prioritize simulation results without losing index correspondence.

Items.Text: np.sort() returns a sorted copy of an array, while np.argsort() returns the indices corresponding to the sorted order.
np.searchsorted() uses binary search to find the index where a value should be inserted to maintain sorted order, enabling efficient lookup.
np.where() returns the indices of elements satisfying a condition, or selects values from two arrays based on a Boolean condition.
np.argmin() and np.argmax() return the indices of the minimum and maximum values along a specified axis.
np.unique() identifies distinct values in an array and can also return counts and inverse indices, useful for categorical engineering data.

Elements.Title: Input/Output and Data Loading Functions
Elements.BodyText: NumPy supports saving and loading array data through a set of I/O functions that interface with binary and text file formats, facilitating data persistence and interoperability in engineering projects. These functions allow engineers to store simulation results and reload experimental datasets efficiently.
Elements.Notes: np.save() and np.load() use NumPy's native .npy binary format for fast and lossless storage of array data, while np.savetxt() and np.loadtxt() handle human-readable CSV or whitespace-delimited files commonly produced by measurement instruments.

Items.Text: np.save() stores a single array to a .npy file, and np.load() retrieves it, preserving dtype and shape exactly.
np.savez() saves multiple arrays into a single compressed .npz archive, reducing storage requirements for large datasets.
np.savetxt() exports array data to a plain text file with configurable delimiters and formatting, suitable for sharing with non-Python tools.
np.loadtxt() and np.genfromtxt() read numerical data from text files, with genfromtxt providing additional handling for missing values.
np.fromstring() and np.frombuffer() create arrays from raw string or buffer data, useful when interfacing with binary data streams from hardware.
